shortDescription: Monthly cash payments for refugees in their first 8 months in the U.S. who are not yet eligible for TANF.
otherCriteria: Income below state TANF limits. Not receiving TANF. Age 18+ (or minor without parent). Must apply within 30 days of arrival or eligibility determination.
timeWindow: Must apply within 8 months of arrival date or date refugee status was granted (whichever is later). Benefit period is up to 8 months.
requiredDocuments: Refugee admission letter or equivalent status document
administeringAgency: State refugee office (funded by federal Office of Refugee Resettlement / ORR)
howToApply: Contact your local resettlement agency or state refugee office. In most states you must apply in person.

shortDescription: Health insurance coverage for refugees who are not eligible for Medicaid, for up to 8 months after arrival.
otherCriteria: Not eligible for or enrolled in Medicaid or CHIP. Within 8 months of arrival or status grant date.
timeWindow: Must apply within 8 months of arrival or status grant date. Coverage lasts up to 8 months.
administeringAgency: State refugee office / state Medicaid agency
howToApply: Apply through your resettlement agency or state refugee office at the same time as RCA.

shortDescription: Free or low-cost health coverage for people with limited income. Refugees and asylees are eligible immediately in most states.
otherCriteria: Income at or below 138% of federal poverty level (in Medicaid expansion states). Pregnant women and children have higher income limits.
timeWindow: No deadline — can apply at any time. Retroactive coverage may be available.
howToApply: Apply online at HealthCare.gov, your state Medicaid website, or through your resettlement agency.

shortDescription: Low-cost health coverage for children in families that earn too much to qualify for Medicaid.
otherCriteria: Child must be under age 19. Family income between Medicaid limit and state CHIP limit (varies by state, often up to 200-300% FPL).
timeWindow: No deadline — can apply at any time.
requiredDocuments: Proof of child's identity and age
howToApply: Apply online at HealthCare.gov or your state CHIP program website.

benefitName: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP / Food Stamps)
shortDescription: Monthly benefits loaded onto an EBT card to buy food. Refugees are eligible immediately; others may have a waiting period.
otherCriteria: Gross income at or below 130% of federal poverty level. Must be a resident of the state where applying. Children, pregnant women, and elderly may have expanded access.
timeWindow: No deadline — can apply at any time. Must reapply periodically (usually every 6-12 months).
requiredDocuments: Proof of immigration status (I-94, status letter)
Proof of income and household expenses
howToApply: Apply at your local SNAP office, online at your state's benefits portal, or through your resettlement agency.

shortDescription: Cash assistance for low-income families with children. Refugees are eligible for the full federal program immediately.
otherCriteria: Must have dependent child(ren) under 18 in household. Income below state TANF limits. Work requirements may apply after an initial period.
timeWindow: No deadline — can apply at any time. Benefits are time-limited (usually 5 years lifetime, varies by state).
howToApply: Apply at your local TANF / social services office or through your state benefits portal.

shortDescription: Nutrition support, healthy food, and breastfeeding help for pregnant women, new mothers, and children up to age 5.
otherCriteria: Must be pregnant, recently gave birth, breastfeeding, or have a child under 5. Income at or below 185% federal poverty level. Must be at nutrition risk (determined at clinic).
timeWindow: No deadline — can apply at any time. Certification periods are 6 months (infants/women) or up to 1 year (children).
requiredDocuments: Proof of pregnancy or child's age
Proof of immigration status (some states do not require this for children)
howToApply: Find your local WIC clinic at WICfinder.fns.usda.gov and call to schedule an appointment.

shortDescription: Monthly cash payments for people who are 65+, blind, or have a disability and have limited income and resources.
otherCriteria: Must be 65 or older, blind, or have a qualifying disability. Limited income and resources. Refugees must apply within 7 years of entry. Asylees have 7 years from grant of asylum.
timeWindow: Refugees and some others must apply within 7 years of entry date. Missing this deadline results in permanent ineligibility.
howToApply: Call SSA at 1-800-772-1213 or visit your local SSA office. Apply as early as possible before the 7-year deadline.

benefitName: Refugee Support Services (RSS) / Matching Grant Program
shortDescription: Employment and self-sufficiency services including job training, English classes, and case management for the first 12 months.
otherCriteria: Must be within 12 months of arrival or status grant. Must be willing to participate in employment services.
timeWindow: Must enroll within 31 days of arrival (Matching Grant) or within 12 months of arrival (Refugee Support Services).
administeringAgency: Voluntary resettlement agencies (VOLAG) contracted by ORR
howToApply: Contact your local resettlement agency immediately upon arrival. Ask about Matching Grant if within 31 days.

shortDescription: A card that proves you are legally allowed to work in the United States.
otherCriteria: Refugees receive EAD automatically with I-94. Asylees must file Form I-765. Asylum seekers (pending) may apply after 180 days of pending case.
timeWindow: Asylum seekers: file Form I-765 after your case has been pending for 180 days. Asylees: file as soon as status is granted. Refugees: should receive automatically, contact resettlement agency if not received.
howToApply: File Form I-765 with USCIS. Fee waivers are available — include Form I-912 if you cannot afford the fee.

shortDescription: A 9-digit number you need to work legally, pay taxes, open a bank account, and access most benefits.
otherCriteria: Must be authorized to work in the U.S. or be receiving certain federal benefits. Refugees should apply immediately upon arrival.
timeWindow: Apply as soon as possible after arrival. Do not delay — many benefits and services require an SSN.
requiredDocuments: Form SS-5 (Application for Social Security Card)
Proof of immigration status and work authorization
howToApply: Visit your local SSA office with your documents. Refugees can apply even before receiving I-94 in some cases.

benefitName: LIHEAP (Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program)
shortDescription: Help paying heating or cooling bills, or energy crisis assistance.
otherCriteria: Household income at or below 150% of federal poverty level or 60% of state median income. Must have an energy cost burden.
timeWindow: Funding is limited and often seasonal — apply early in fall for heating assistance or spring for cooling assistance.
howToApply: Apply through your state or local LIHEAP office. Contact 211 or your resettlement agency for local office information.

shortDescription: Refugees must apply for a green card after 1 year in the U.S. Asylees may apply after 1 year of asylum status.
otherCriteria: Refugees: must apply exactly 1 year after arrival (this is mandatory by law). Asylees: may apply after 1 year of asylum status. Missing the refugee deadline can affect future citizenship eligibility.
timeWindow: Refugees: MUST file Form I-485 within 1 year of U.S. entry (federal law). Asylees: SHOULD file Form I-485 within 1 year of asylum grant. Critically time-sensitive.
requiredDocuments: Form I-485 (Application to Register Permanent Residence)
Refugee travel document or other status document
Filing fee (fee waivers may be available)
howToApply: File Form I-485 with USCIS. Fee waivers available with Form I-912. A licensed attorney or DOJ-accredited representative can help.

shortDescription: If you are in the U.S. and fear persecution if returned to your country, you may apply for asylum.
otherCriteria: Must be physically present in the U.S. Must file within 1 year of arriving in the U.S. (extremely important deadline). Exceptions exist for changed or extraordinary circumstances.
timeWindow: MUST file Form I-589 within 1 year of arriving in the U.S. Missing this deadline results in permanent ineligibility unless exceptions apply. This is one of the most critical deadlines in immigration law.
requiredDocuments: Evidence of persecution or fear of persecution
administeringAgency: U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) — affirmative; Immigration Court (EOIR) — defensive
howToApply: File Form I-589 with USCIS within 1 year of arrival. Strongly recommended to work with a licensed attorney or DOJ-accredited representative.

shortDescription: All children, regardless of immigration status, have the right to enroll in public K-12 schools under federal law (Plyler v. Doe).
otherCriteria: Child must be of school age (generally 5-18, varies by state). Schools cannot require immigration documents for enrollment.
timeWindow: Enroll as soon as possible — children have the right to immediate enrollment. Schools may not delay enrollment for missing immunization records, birth certificates, or other documents.
requiredDocuments: Proof of residency in the school district (utility bill, lease, etc.)
Immunization records (if available — school must enroll without them)
Academic records from previous school (if available)
howToApply: Contact your local school district's enrollment office. If refused, contact your state Department of Education or an immigration legal aid organization.
